g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Packages in Java! We'll explore how packages organize code, prevent conflicts, and modularize applications.
A package is a logical grouping of related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages. It organizes code and prevents naming conflicts between classes.
g.text: A logical grouping mechanism for related

g.data-narration: Packages provide three key benefits: namespace management to avoid conflicts, access control for encapsulation, and modularization for maintainability.
The package declaration uses the 'package' keyword and must be the first statement in your Java file. The name must match the directory structure.
A package can contain four types of components: classes, interfaces, enumerations, and sub-packages. All files must share the same package declaration.
g.text: All source files in a package
must have the SAME package declaration

g.data-narration: Package names must directly correspond to folder structure. The package 'com.myapp.utils' means you need folder path 'com/myapp/utils/' containing your .java files.
Use the 'import' keyword to access classes from other packages. Specific import loads one class, while wildcard (*) imports all classes from a package.
Java provides powerful built-in packages: java.lang for fundamentals, java.util for collections, java.io for I/O, java.awt for GUI, and java.net for networking.
A class without any package declaration belongs to the default package. This unnamed package is suitable only for small programs and not for production applications.
Package names follow reverse domain naming: company domain 'example.com' becomes 'com.example'. Use all lowercase letters with dots separating words.
g.text: Reverse Domain Name: company domain → package prefix

g.data-narration: A fully qualified name includes the complete package path plus the class name. For example, ArrayList in java.util becomes 'java.util.ArrayList'.
g.text: Package path + Class name = Fully Qualified Name

g.data-narration: To create a user-defined package: 1) Create folder matching package name, 2) Add Java source file, 3) Declare package at top, 4) Compile with javac.
Here's a complete example: declaring a package 'com.mypackage', creating a class, then compiling and running it using the fully qualified name.
Key exam points: package declaration must be first, include root directory in classpath, don't confuse import with C++ include, and remember FQN usage.
In summary: packages organize code, prevent conflicts, and enable modular design. Use package declarations, import wisely, and follow naming conventions for clean Java code.
